content.full_text:
August 1, 1977


Mr. Frank H. Martinez
1942 DeLaPena Avenue
Santa Clara, CA 95050

Dear Mr. Martinez:

Your letter of May 10, 1977 addressed to the Secretary of
State's Office was referred to our office on July 11, 1977.
Your questions concern your obligations and prohibitions
under the Political Reform Act as a labor union.

I will attempt to answer your questions in the sequence in
which they occur.

1. There are no restrictions under the law governing the
amount of money which may be contributed to candidates,
ballot measures or the qualification of a measure for
the ballot. A check should be made with a local jurisdiction to determine whether or not there is a city or
county ordinance which limits contributions or expenditures for local elections.

2. There is no prohibition concerning the use of the union's
fund money for making political contributions.

3. The state law imposes no restriction on the use of lists
for candidates. I would suggest, however, you consult
your union's bylaws in an effort to determine whether or
not the use of these lists for the purpose you outline
would be a violation.

I have enclosed some informational material which may be of
some assistance to you in determining what your obligations
under the law may be. If you wish to consult a knowledgeable
source locally, I suggest that you contact the office of
County Clerk, Registrar of Voters for Santa Clara County,
299-2161. Mr. Mann's office has the responsibility to administer
the law locally and I am certain he will be happy to help you
in any way possible.

Very truly yours,

Regina Siciliano-Kutchina
Program Coordinator

RSK/gt

July 6, 1977

Mr. Frank H. Martinez
1942 DeLaPena Avenue
Santa Clara, California 95050

Dear Mr. Martinez:

I am responding to your letter to the Secretary of
State dated May 10th, relating to political activities of
labor unions.

The California Elections Code contains no prohibition
against a labor organization's leadership from using the
labor organization's membership list to make political
mailings. If the California Labor Code, or other law,
which we have not researched, contains no such prohibition,
then I would think the labor organization's by-laws or
labor contract would govern such mailings.

The state's Fair Political Practices Commission is the
best source of information on campaign expenditure limits,
and I would suggest that you contact their Technical
Assistance Division for information on how expenditure
limits would apply to a local union affiliated with a
national union.

I am forwarding your letter to the F.P.P.C. for
consideration of your expenditure question, and I would
suggest that you contact them for further information.

Very truly yours,

WILLIAM N. DURLEY
Assistant to the Secretary of State
Elections and Political Reform


RICHARD B. MANESS
Legal Counsel
Elections and Political Reform

RBM:gb

cc: Fair Political Practices Commission

1942 DelaPena Ave.
Santa Clara, Cal. 95050
May 10, 1977

California Secretary of State
Honorable March Fong Yu
State Capitol Bldg.
Sacramento, Cal. 95814

Madam:

I'm writing you this letter to request from you all pertinent
information pertaining to the do and don'ts in the California
political circles by labor unions.

Specifically, what amounts of money can a local union contribute to a political candidate, or campaign issues such as bonds,
or initiatives on voters ballots that are beneficial to their
membership.

Secondly, can this money come from the general fund of the union
or does special funds have to be raised by groups of members
separate from union affiliation.

Thirdly, can the [illegible] elected representatives of the membership
use the addresses and phone numbers of members on file; for the
purpose of organizing community groups for the purpose of educating them to issues that are in our governmental legislative
process that are beneficial or detrimental to their well-being.

My reason for the above approach is because I have found that
when you speak to a group on a personal basis you receive a
better response and being chairman of the organizational and
political action committee in our local; response on issues from
our members is very important to me and my committeemen.

I have approached my Secretary Treasurer on my scheme to generate
response and interest from our membership on issues concerning
their welfare. His response has been that releasing names and
phone numbers of members to anyone including the committee is unlawful. He insist that this approach violates the privacy act.
I say he's playing cheap politics and in turn doing a great disservice to the membership; would you please furnish the correct
information that will prove me right, or wrong.

Sincerely yours,

Frank H. Martinez

Cfm/on

[illegible stamps and dates at bottom]
